Action: The career officials have resigned in protest over DOGE practices, saying "We will not use our skills as technologists to compromise core government systems, jeopardize Americans’ sensitive data, or dismantle critical public services."
Resistance: 21 Career Employees of DOGE (formerly the US Digital Service) resigned from the service, writing "we swore to serve the American people and uphold our oath to the Constitution across presidential administrations. However, it has become clear that we can no longer honor those commitments at the United States DOGE Service.”

The union argues the administration's order has politicized the civil service.
column9: What have we will document: Attack on DEI's policy, immigration and deporations, education, foreign aid, media attacks, FBI, erasure of knowledge, democratic backsliding, judicial changes, private information access, staff purges, dismantle of institutions and individuals rights

Source: How major law firms are responding to Trump’s attacks - POLITICO
Citation (MLA): Barnes, Daniel. "How Major Law Firms Are Responding to Trump’s Attacks." Politico, 19 Mar. 2025, https://www.politico.com/news/2025/03/19/trump-major-law-firm-sanctions-questions-00236446.
Action: This article points to a mostly muted response among Big Law firms against government crackdown, showing a fearful; civil society. However, the continued litigation against administration policies shows a scared, but perhaps relatively strong civil society.
Resistance: Since taking office, Trump has signed multiple executive orders targetting large law firms that had worked on behalf of his perceived political enemies. As a result, many of these firms fear further retribution from the administration, and few leaders of major law firms have released statements criticizing the administration. However, associates at dozens of law firms have begun circulating an open letter against the administration's crackdown, with 300 associates having anonymously signed the letter since it went live a week ago. Some major law firms continue to litigate against administration policies, while some have begun litigating for the administration.

Source: How a Major Democratic Law Firm Ended Up Bowing to Trump - NYT
Citation (MLA): Schmidt, Michael S., Matthew Goldstein, Jessica Silver-Greenberg, and Ben Protess. “Paul Weiss Deal With Trump Faces Backlash From Legal Profession.” The New York Times, 21 Mar. 2025, https://www.nytimes.com/2025/03/22/us/politics/paul-weiss-trump.html.
Action: While Paul Weiss' deal helps their firm maintain business, it sets a poor precedent for other law firms. It demonstrates that law firms may be willing to negotiate with the President--leaving civil society and leading law firms vulnerable to further intimidation. It also demonstrates a weakness among civil society will to resist against the administrations retributive campaign.
Resistance: After Trump revoked the security clearances of Paul Weiss lawyers, the firm initially planned to sue. However, after discussions, the law firm Chariman met with President Trump, agreeing to a deal to maintain their clearances. The firm apparently pledged 40 million in pro-bono work for causes the President has championed, and may have also agreed to end DEI practices and acknowledged that a political enemy of Trump, and former Paul Weiss partner had committed "wrongdoing."

Source: Columbia student protester sues U.S. to prevent deportation - WaPo
Citation (MLA): Vinall, Frances. "Columbia Student Yunseo Chung Sues Trump Administration Over Deportation." The Washington Post, 25 Mar. 2025, https://www.washingtonpost.com/nation/2025/03/25/trump-lawsuit-ice-columbia-yunseo-chung.
Action: This lawsuit represents resistance on behalf of Yunseo Cheng, a private citizen and US legal resident, seeking to prevent her arrest and deportation. This result of this lawsuit will prove extremely significant in the administration's push to silence critics.
Resistance: Yunseo Chenga, a Columbia Student and immigrant from South Korea, has sued the administration, arguing that her demonstrations are protected by the first ammendment. Chung was arrested after participating in a demonstration and charged with obstruction of governmental administration. Days later, ICE agents informed her that her legal status was being revoked. A federal judge issued a temporary restraining order blocking authorities from detaining or deporting her.

Source: Law firms refuse to represent Trump opponents in the wake of his attacks - WaPo
Citation (MLA): Birnbaum, M. (2025, March 25). Law firms refuse to represent Trump opponents in the wake of his attacks. The Washington Post. https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2025/03/25/trump-law-firms/
Action: This represents a threat to the Constitutional right to due process and legal representation. Additionally, by targetting lawyers, the Trump administraion is weakening civil society's capacity to challenge his actions in court--which can carry large ramifications for democracy. This appears to violate democratic norms and the constitution.
Resistance: Biden-era officials, volunteers, and non-profits have all reported trouble finding legal counsel. This follows the Trump administration's actions against high-profile law firms that have assisted his perceived political enemies. Legal scholars argue that no administration has targetted the legal establishment to this extent.

Action: A significant protest against the Trump administration's arrest of Mahmoud Khalil.
Resistance: 300 protesters entered Trump Tower and swarmed the ground floor in a demonstration demanding the release of Mahmoud Khalil. 98 protesters were arrested and charged by the NYPD with misdemeanors. No injuries were sustained and no property was damaged.

Action: This article highlights the different approaches law firms are taking in response to targeting by the Trump administration. Some are opting to sue in court, while other are negotiating with the President. These different approaches carry major ramifications for civil society's capacity to resist and indicate civil society will.
Resistance: Jenner & Block and WilmerHale have filed lawsuits against the Trump administration, alleging that its Executive Order targetting the law firms violates the First Amendment. Around the same time, President Trump announced that a third law firm, Skadden, agreed to provide 100 million in pro bono funding for causes approved by the administration. Since taking office, the Trump administration has targetted five major law firms, stripping security clearances and government access.

Source: Universities Sprint From ‘We Will Not Cower’ to Appeasing Trump - WSJ
Citation (MLA): Severns, Maggies. "Universities, Trump, Lobbyists, and Funding in Washington." The Wall Street Journal, 25 Mar. 2025, https://www.wsj.com/us-news/education/universities-trump-lobbyists-funding-washington-a2e5c77a
Action: This article highlights a lack of resistance among Universities against the Trump administration and shows the impact of the administration's campaign. Universities can often be a source of civil society resistance during times of democratic backsliding or government overreach, but the Trump administration's targeting of higher-education institutions appear to have silenced much of civil society's colleges and universities.
Resistance: As a result of the Trump administration's targetted campaign against higher education institutions, colleges, and universities have mostly abandoned their attempts to resist and have attempted to appease the administration. Columbia University has agreed to a long list of conditions with the administration in an attempt to retain federal funding, while multiple schools, including Stanford, Duke, Wake Forest, Harvard, and Vanderbilt, have hired high-ranking former Republicans lawmakers or advisors as lobbyists. Meanwhile, Republicans are continuing their push against education institutions, as lawmakers propose taxes on endowments or loan repayment programs.

Citation (MLA): Collins, Dave. “Angry Protesters from New York to Alaska Assail Trump and Musk in ‘hands off!’ Rallies.” AP News, AP News, 7 Apr. 2025, apnews.com/article/trump-musk-doge-protests-hands-off-472c574303260cbac315367cc808960d?utm_source=copy&utm_medium=share.
Action: The widespread protests represent the first major mass mobilization movement of the Trump Presidency, and demonstrate some level of civil society strength. Demonstrations and protests are a major part of civil society, as they can represent the power of active citizens.
Resistance: In over 1200 locations across 50 states, demonstrators have conducted so-called "Hand-off" protests to oppose the Trump administration. The demonstrations included over 150+ groups, and in some locations, Democratic lawmakers spoke. The demonstrations was the first mass-mobilization movement of Trump's second term, and represented a number of causes including, but not limited to, social security, immigration, democracy, transgender rights, opposition to DOGE, and government firings.

Resistance: Harvard's resistance to Trump is the biggest of any college or university since Trump took office. The Trump Administration wants to control Harvard's ‘intellectual conditions,’ while also banning DEI programs and creating policies such as a campus-wide mask ban. Harvard argues that this infringes on its First Amendment Rights and disregards due process. Therefore, it has not implemented any changes.

Source: The Ivy League resistance is just getting started - POLITICO
Citation (MLA): Quilantan, Bianca, and Madina Touré. "Former Presidents of Columbia, Harvard Urge Their Successors to Fight the Trump Administration." Politico, 17 Apr. 2025, https://www.politico.com/news/2025/04/17/columbia-harvard-former-presidents-urge-successors-fight-trump-00294325.
Action: This article shows a more motivated and prepared civil society than we have seen before, following Columbia's decision to acquiesce to the Trump administration's demands. The effectiveness of either of these responses will have far-reaching ramifications for civil society's will to resist.
Resistance: After Harvard's resistance toward the Trump administration's targeting, several former Ivy League Presidents have called on higher-education institutions to resist the Trump administration's demands, specifically through courts. They argue it is morally important and also the only effective response.

Source: Five More Big Law Firms Reach Deals With Trump - NYT
Citation (MLA): Goldstein, Matthew. "Five More Big Law Firms Reach Deals With Trump." The New York Times, 11 Apr. 2025, www.nytimes.com/2025/04/11/business/trump-law-firms-kirkland-ellis-latham-watkins.html.
Action: This article shows the Trump Administration's continued targeting of major law firms and the different strategies those law firms have taken in response. The Trump administration's pressure campaign against law firms appears to violate the constitutional right to legal representation and represents an unprecedented crackdown on civil society dissent. The article highlights some firms' decisions to negotiate and strike deals with law firms, while others have filed lawsuits.
Resistance: Five majors law firms; Kirkland & Ellis, Latham and Watkins, A&O Shearman; Simpson Thacher & Bartlett; and Wickersham & Taft; have agreed to deals with the Trump administration to provide a total of 600 million dollars worth of pro bono legal work for the administration. Some law firms are taking matters to court, and Judges have temporarily stayed the orders against Perkins Coie, WilmerHale, and Jenner & Block. Another firm, Susan Godfrey, has also filed suit.

Source: Harvard Sues the Trump Administration- NYT
Citation (MLA): Saul, Stephanie. “Harvard University Sues Trump Administration.” The New York Times, 21 Apr. 2025. NYTimes.com, https://www.nytimes.com/2025/04/21/us/harvard-lawsuit-trump-administration.html.
Action: This article shows continued resistance by Harvard in the face of legal and financial pressures from the Trump administration. Due to the institution's wealth, they have the flexibility to take on this lawsuit, though they have asked the Massachusetts court to expedite the proceeding. The University's president has spoken out in multiple community letters advocating for the institution's first ammendment rights.
Resistance: In recent weeks, the Trump administration has demanded Haravrd institute new policies including "auditing professors for plagiarism, reporting to the federal government any international students accused of misconduct, and appointing an outside overseer to insure that academic departments were 'viewpoint diverse'," as well as threatening to take away their SEVP status, which allows them to admit international students." Harvard's President, Alan M. Garber, called these demands “unprecedented and improper” with potential for “severe and long lasting” consequences.

Protesters chained themselves to Columbia's gates and staged a sit-in. Demonstrators chanted slogans, held signs, and read messages from detention.

Action: This shows a strengthened civil society in the academic sphere, with an effort to unite and resist the Trump administration's pressure and crackdown. A united front would make it difficult for the Trump administration to back academic institutions into a corner and force them into unbalanced agreements.
Resistance: Privately, leaders of America's most prestigious universities have assembled a private collective to resist the Trump administration's targeting. The group comprises figures at high levels, including individuals and trustees at about 10 schools, including Ivies and leading private research institutions.    The group has coordinated resistance, drawing red lines in negotiations and formulating plans of how to respond to different administration demands.  This collective is working to coordinate dealmaking so that no university strikes a deal with the administration that would create a worrisome precedent for academic independence.

Action: The deal challenges the University's independence and grants legitimacy to the President's campaign to weaponize research funding in an effort to transform University politics and campus unrest. Academic independence is an integral part of civil society, and Columbia's decision to settle risks the civil society to resist democratic backsliding.
Resistance: Columbia has reached a major deal with the Trump administration to restore the university's research funding. In exchange, the University will pay a $200 million fine, pledge to follow laws banning the consideration of race in admission and hiring, and follow through on other commitments to reduce antisemitism and unrest that it agreed to in March. The deal is the first by a University targetted by the administration's anti-semitism and DEI investigation into Universities.

Action: As the article adds, the investigation and potential settlement are part of the Trump administration's pressure campaign against elite universities. Higher-education institutions serve an integral role in democracy by serving as grounds for political discourse and historical recordkeeping. Their independence has long been respected in America, and a settlement is noteworthy.
Resistance: Yale University is reportedly pursuing a settlement with the US Government over the Trump administration's investigation into whether Yale University's admissions practices violate anti-discrimination laws.
